turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments first this morning, in Number 79-1344; Michael M. against the Superior Court of Sonoma County, California. Now, Mr. Jilka, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: My name is Gregory Jilka. I am one of the attorneys for Michael M., a 17-year old California youth who comes before this Court seeking reversal of the judgment of the Supreme Court of California, a judgment which upheld the validity, the constitutional validity of California Penal Code Section 261.5, on equal protection grounds. In oral argument this morning, I propose first to outline the issue in its procedural posture; secondly, to discuss the purposes and to assert the purposes of the statute and thirdly, to discuss the so-called pregnancy prevention rationale and as to whether or not it really diminishes the criminal culpability of the female. At issue in this case is the right of California to prosecute my client, Michael, for violation of the statute. The statute provides, and I quote, it prohibits "an act of sexual intercourse accomplished with a female not the wife of the perpetrator, where the female is under the age of 18 years. " The operative facts in this case are simply that Michael stands accused of the crime. He has not been convicted of the crime and comes before this Court clothed with the presumption of innocence. He is currently awaiting trial, pending the decision of the Court in this case. Both California and the United States, as amicus on behalf of California, have suggested to this Court that Michael is guilty of a crime of which he is not charged; that crime is forcible rape. The suggestion that force existed in this case is unsupported by any judicial finding and is immaterial to the issues presented. And, in my opinion, tends to obfuscate the issue.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: What was the charge? I mean, could the charge possibly embrace forcible as well as statutory?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: No, Justice Rehnquist, it could not. The information which is contained in the appendix is the charging document and it clearly states that Michael is charged with violation of the statute and the elements are simply that he participated in an act of sexual intercourse with a female who was under the age of 18. Forcible rape would not be a lesser included offense.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So, as you say, the suggestion that force was used is irrelevant because the issue before us is the constitutional validity of the statute on its face--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: --is that correct? So it's equally irrelevant that Michael was 17 years old?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: He was not... I mean, if you're right on the first--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: --the second follows.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I do believe, however, that we have fairly presented a limited constitutional question on the applicability of the statute as applied to a 17 and a half year old female who is charged with sexual intercourse with a 16 and a half year old.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I feel that those facts are fairly before the Court because--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I thought that Michael was a male?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Yes, I'm sorry, Your Honor. He is, he certainly is. Those facts were alleged in the petition for writ of mandate, and were admitted by the State of California.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well if we're going to get into the facts of this particular case, then the... and if this is a question of the constitutionality of the statute as applied, then both the matter that you've been discussing and the age of your client are, perhaps, relevant. But if the issue is the constitutional validity of this statute on its face, then neither the alleged facts of this episode, nor the age of the Defendant, are really relevant, are they?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, as far as the facial issue of constitutionality goes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Counsel, could I ask you a question about the California situation? Could he have been charged... well, was he charged with a felony?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. He was charged with a felony.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Could he have been charged for a misdemeanor?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, this statute could have been charged as a misdemeanor in the discretion of the district attorney, the prosecuting authority. If convicted, Michael--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Jilka, could I interrupt again, because I'm always--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: --somewhat puzzled when we talk about constitutionality as applied or on the face, the case arises out of... doesn't it really involve a test of the legal sufficiency or constitutionality of the specific complaint filed against this man?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: So, don't we have to take as true, for purposes of decision, whatever facts are alleged therein? Assuming that--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that's a fair statement.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: --they will prove those and that they will not have to prove anything not alleged therein?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's true. I would, however, point out that what happened at the trial court was not in the nature of a demurrer, but it was a motion to dismiss, at which evidence was presented and primarily the evidence which was presented was the age of Michael. So--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well that is not... the age is not in the complaint itself?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --That's correct, it is not part of the complaint, but it is certainly a matter of record. If convicted, Michael's conviction would be predicated on the existence of essentially four facts. First of all, that Michael is a male; secondly, that he simply participated in an act of sexual intercourse, that Sharon, his partner, was under the age of 18 years, and fourth, that Michael and Sharon were not married.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Counsel, I am troubled, as apparently some of my colleagues are, by the rather abstract question that's presented. Ordinarily, if a motion to dismiss and information is denied, the case goes to trial. And we have testimony and a verdict, or judges' findings, and it goes on, up through appeal to the state system and we get it here with a specific batch of facts, so to speak. You are simply challenging the statute on its face?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's essentially correct, Justice Rehnquist. The California Supreme Court, in exercise of its original and discretionary jurisdiction, issued an alternative writ to call that issue before it and decided that very issue of the facial constitutionality of the statute. So when the case comes here, fortunately or unfortunately, it's really without a record below or a very, very limited record.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: And all the California Supreme Court said was that, on its face this statute is constitutional?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I believe that's an accurate synopsis of the California Supreme Court's opinion. The statute, California Penal Code Section 261.5, embodies and reflects the traditional sexual stereotypes. Because only the male can violate the statute, it is presumed that he is the passionate aggressor, and similarly, conversely, that the female is the helpless victim who needs the paternalistic protection of the state and the protection of the statute. Neither California nor the United States have even attempted to refute that the statute is cast in terms of the traditional and outmoded sex roles.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, but there... there is one thing that isn't outmoded, isn't it, and that is that women can have children and men can't?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that's certainly true.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Women can become pregnant and men can't?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That is absolutely true, of course.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is it your basic... since I've already interrupted you, is it your basic contention that California could have enacted a law such as this, if it applied not only to both men and women, but to people of all ages, victims of all ages?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: As far as the age criteria goes, that's not challenged. What we're challenging is the discrimination between males and females.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, then is it... is it your submission that California could have enacted a law such as this if it had applied to victims of both sexes?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Under 17?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Although the next claim would be that it was unconstitutional for California to do that for people under 17, when it didn't do it for people over 17?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's... that certainly could be made, Your Honor. I think that that would be a much different case because the discrimination--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It would be a different case, but it would be a constitutional challenge, wouldn't it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --It could be, it certainly could be. In that case, there would not be discrimination, of course, on the basis of sex.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So it's your claim that California, having... cannot enact this for the protection of women alone; that if it enacts this, it has to go the whole hog and enact it for the protection of all people of either sex under 17 years old?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. And I believe that has been done in varying forms by at least 37 of the states.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Wouldn't it follow that a state that passed a law to protect the purchasers of automobiles would have to pass a law to protect the purchasers of dishwashers?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well no, Your Honor, I don't believe so. Certainly not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well isn't that pretty much what your argument is?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: No. Your Honor. In this case we're talking about classifications on the basis of sex, which perpetuate we feel, the traditional stereotypes. Because--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: California, if it tries to protect females, must protect males also?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Similarly situated males, yes--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That the constitution requires it to do so?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor, that is our contention.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Yes. Then, that would be true of forcible rape as well as statutory rape?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well that certainly could be one of the logical conclusions of the invalidity of the statute that's challenged. I think that, when force is involved, there certainly is a much different situation before the Court. But that's... that argument has certainly been made, and I believe, in a recent Tulane Law School Law Review article that that very hypothesis is set forward.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's right. But I do not believe that this Court's invalidation of the statute would necessarily force the conclusion that all forcible rape laws are gender neutral, or must be gender neutral. In fact, in California, the forcible rape law is gender neutral, ironically enough, and so are all of the other statutes that are designed to prevent the sexual exploitation of California citizens.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Can women be guilty of the offense under this California statute as aiders and abettors?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, that's correct. As a racial minority--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Accessories?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Yes, that's right, that's right, they could, certainly, but not as a principal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Can the state... can a state constitutionally make it a criminal act for a woman of, let us say, 30, to seduce a young boy of 14 or 15?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I would certainly say that that's within the realm of the state's legitimate concern--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Most states do, do they not?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Yes, in one form or another; for example, in California, it is a very minor offense, it's contributing to the delinquency of a minor, it's a misdemeanor. Whereas Michael, because... simply because of his sex, now is facing a felony charge. If the sex roles were reversed around, if it were an older male or a male of any age and a female, there's heightened criminal penalties.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You may have answered this before, but if he had been charged with a misdemeanor instead of a felony, would you be here?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I don't know. The procedure that was utilized in the Supreme Court of California, exercising its original jurisdiction is predicated in the statute and I just don't know if the California Supreme Court would have taken this case had it been in a lower level court; that's problematic.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Jilka, can I pursue a question that Mr. Justice Stewart asked you about washing machines and cars? Did you focus entirely on protection of the victim being sex neutral or are you also focusing at all on the punishment? In other words, are you in effect arguing that it would be unconstitutional to have a statute that said it's unlawful for men to steal cars but there is no prohibition against women stealing cars?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I think that would follow, Your Honor; certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: That's more, that's your position? But you told us that women can be guilty of violating this statute?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: As an aider and abettor, that's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And California, does... does California generally treat aiders and abettors the same as principals, as far as sentences go?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I guess I could not, in honesty, answer that question.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Do you know the federal criminal law generally does?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, well, the penalties that are prescribed are the same for aiders and abettors as for the principals, certainly, if that's your question, yes. I would point out to the case, a new Eighth Circuit opinion in a case called Navedo v. Preisser, which was decided on September 22nd of this year, in which the Eighth Circuit invalidated the Iowa statutory rape law. That was a different statutory rape law than California, certainly, but I think that some of the reasoning certainly applies here. In considering the pregnancy--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That case is not in your brief, I gather?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --No, Your Honor, it's a recent case; it was after the brief, it was decided September 22nd, 1980, in the Eighth Circuit.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: What's the style of it, the citation?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Navedo, N-a-v-e-d-o v. Preisser, and I could provide the Court with the case number.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Will you do that?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I certainly will, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Preisser?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, it's P-r-e-i-s-s-e-r, is the second name. The first is Navedo, N-a-v-e-d-o.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Eighth Circuit?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: September?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: September 22nd.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Of this year. Thank you. Mr. Jilka, excuse me again for interrupting. Mr. Justice Stewart made the point about aiders and abettors and you said they are equally culpable and equally punishable. Doesn't that entirely destroy your case?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well because,--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: What's the discrimination if both the male and the female can be punished equally and there can't be a crime unless they are both liable for some kind of punishment?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well a male and a female can be punished equally only if the female aids and abets the male in performing an act of sexual intercourse with a female under the age of 18. There is no... what we're focusing on here should be the conduct. If a male engages in sexual intercourse with a female under 18, that's a felony; but if a female does not do the very same... if the female does the very same act, that is, engages in sexual intercourse with a--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: It seems to me by hypothesis it's either voluntary or involuntary on the part of the female; and if it's involuntary, why then you've got a forcible rape situation.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --That's certainly correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And you're not challenging that?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: If it's voluntary, then don't you, by hypothesis, have an aiding and abetting situation?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I suppose so, Your Honor. However, I would represent to the Court that in practice, that the victim, the purported victim is never charged with a crime. I couldn't--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well I suppose a good many men who violate the statute are not charged with it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Oh, I would certainly agree with that, Your Honor. There are... we estimate that there are probably more--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So that's no answer, in other words.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So your answer to my brother Stevens was really no answer to his question. Moreover I imagine that in these states that have sex neutral statutes like this, probably most of the prosecutions are males anyway.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I don't know that to be true, Your Honor, but that certainly could be. I suppose the point that I was trying to make was that the engaging in sexual conduct... sexual intercourse for a male and a female is basically the same act, a mutual act; for a male under the same circumstances it's a felony, but for the female to participate, it is not. And I do not believe that a female can and would be charged as an aider and abettor, a female so-called victim.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: There is a case in the federal system that said that the so-called victim in a Mann Act prosecution, could be guilty of conspiracy of her own... to carrying on her own transportation in interstate commerce for immoral purposes; wouldn't the same principal apply?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I don't believe that it has been applied Your Honor, and I... in California, Your Honor... and I don't have the case authority for that, I certainly wish I did. But I believe that to be the case, that the female is in practice and in law not considered to be culpable as an aider and abettor. That is the female victim, excuse me.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Would your case still survive if California had a statute that, contributing to the delinquency of a minor boy was a felony?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: It may, it may.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: When? What doubt do you have about it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well the doubt that I would have would be if the substantive elements of the crime were identical and the purposes were identical, then arguably there would be no discrimination against males, or there would be no unequal treatment of the sexes, but that is not the case--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: If the range of sentences were the same for both the present statute and the one I hypothesized would there by any difference?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --If the sentences were the same and the substantive elements of the crime were the same, I think that we would have great difficulty with an equal protection tack... I agree. I would point out to the Court that the California case law resoundingly demonstrates that the motivation for the enactment of the statute is the concern for the virtue of young girls and not the pregnancy prevention rationale. I would point out the cases cited at pages 25 and 26 of the brief of the Petitioner. These cases span the range of years 1895 through 1975--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Of course, Your Supreme Court has said otherwise recently, hasn't it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --The Supreme Court has recently said otherwise in this case, but--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Aren't we bound by that?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --No, Your Honor, this Court is not bound by that and this Court has the right and in fact, the duty, to inquire into the purposes of the statute where the purposes reflect upon a constitutional claim. Otherwise, the state courts, by--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Even when the Supreme Court of California pronounces what the purpose of their statute was?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --That's correct, Your Honor. That is correct. This Court has the duty to look into the--

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well how could we know any better than the Supreme Court of California or the California legislature what the purpose of enacting a statute was?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well in answer to that, I would say that the burden of demonstrating the purpose of the statute is on the government in this case and that--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You mean the presumption of constitutionality doesn't attend to the statutes in California?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --There is a... certainly a presumption of constitutionality, Your Honor, but I believe under the cases in this Court, the Wengler case, that it's been stated that the burden is on the state to make out the claim for the justification of stature. I believe that applies here. And I don't think the Court will have--

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: What's the difference between the presumption of constitutionality of the statute and the burden of the state to make out the justification for it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well I believe that the presumption of constitutionality is a broad presumption which applies to all statutes in general. But once, in a case, discrimination on the basis of sex is made out, then the burden shifts to the government to justify the classifications of the statute. I believe that that was the reasoning of the Ninth Circuit adopted in the case of the United States v. Hicks, which was cited in our brief, a recent Ninth Circuit case which invalidated a federal statute which--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Do you know, has the government taken appeal on that one?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --It's my information that the government has filed a petition for a writ of certiorari; I'm sorry, I don't know the number of it, but it was filed approximately two weeks ago, and it's also my information that the government has asked this Court to refrain from taking any action in the Hicks case until the disposition of this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Let me go back to my inquiry about the California courts' observation of the purposes of this statute. I take it it's your position that having said in those older cases what the purpose was, that that's engraved in stone, the Supreme Court of California now may not look at the same material and come to a different conclusion.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well not exactly, Mr. Justice Blackmun. The most recent California Supreme Court case is only 1964, it's not an old case. The most recent Court of Appeals case is 1975, that also is not an old case. It's not that the justifications are--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: You're arguing calendar, then?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well that's certainly part of it, Your Honor. But the pregnancy prevention rationale as pointed out in the Meloon case out of the First Circuit, is suspect, and that the recent Eighth Circuit case, the Eighth Circuit speaks in terms of pregnancy prevention as being a suspect justification because it's so readily used and so readily available to justify discrimination on the basis of sex.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Of course what does the Eighth Circuit case have to do with the purpose of the California statute?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: It has nothing to do with the purpose of the California statute, except insofar--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: I'm just troubled by this apparent reversal of your Supreme Court as to the purpose of the California Court, and I am troubled, when I ask it doesn't mean that I'm fixed on my answer to it--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: --as to whether we are not bound by your interpretation of the purpose of their statute.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: I believe in the cases cited in my brief, Mr. Justice Blackmun, that we have adequately cited authority for the proposition that this Court does have the obligation and the duty to look beyond the state court's determination of what the purposes of the statute is.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well what real difference does it make what the legislative purpose was? I know that a good deal of time was spent by both you and your brother, in your briefs, in talking about the purpose but isn't it the duty of this Court to look at the statute on its face and to, depending upon what level of so-called scrutiny one wants to apply, to uphold the statute if a rational basis can be conceived for its existence?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well, Your Honor--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Isn't it a pretty elusive quest to try to find out what the purpose of the California legislature was, back at the time of the enactment of this statute, maybe the various members of the legislature who voted for the statute had a variety of purposes?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Certainly, yes. However, we would contend and we have contended that the standard of Craig v. Boren is applicable in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Regardless of the original purpose or the present purpose or whatever this California Supreme Court said about its purpose, the duty of this Court is to look at the statute and its impact and, depending upon, as I say, the level of so-called scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause to decide whether or not the statute is rationally based. Isn't that about it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well, essentially, Your Honor, I believe there is language in the Court's opinions in the past that do delve into the purposes of the statutes and whether or not the classifications served are substantially related to those purposes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is there any legislative history of this state legislation that one can look at?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Essentially none.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: No, I didn't think so.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: There really is none. All we have is the California Court opinions, basically.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Most states have no legislative history of the kind that we find in the Congress, generally, isn't that so?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: That is my understanding.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: So does that have some connection with the general proposition that if there is any rational basis which the Court can see for the legislation, then there is an obligation to sustain it?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well I think as a general proposition that that is certainly correct. However, because discrimination in this case is based upon sex that the burden is on the government to make out the claimed justification. Of course, our argument here is that this is based on heightened scrutiny because of the disparate effect that this has on California teenagers particularly.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Disparate effect as between teenaged boys and teenaged girls, or--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: In this case, certainly; and as between males and females of all ages who essentially engage in the same kind of conduct, be it reprehensible or not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: --But as I understood your answer to one of my earlier questions, you don't attack this statute on the basis that it protects people only under 17 years old?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: No, that's correct. That is correct. If it please the Court, I'd like to--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It punishes males of any age, does it not?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well do you think you are fatally wounded if we accept the purpose of the statute as announced by the State Supreme Court in this case? Justice Mosk didn't seem to think so.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't think that we would be fatally wounded. I think that it would be impossible for California to demonstrate that the purpose of the statute prevention of pregnancy is substantially related to... or that the classification of the statute is substantially related to that purpose because it's quite overinclusive... it punishes conduct which cannot result in pregnancy.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, what if it is overinclusive?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well the test, as I understand it, is that the classifications have to be substantially related. In this case, sex, the male gender is essentially used as a proxy for culpability, and we contend that that's an imperfect proxy. There is a weak congruence.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, assume there is an equal culpability, but unequal impact on the parties engaging in this conduct; which is what, I take it, the California Court relied upon, and that apparently drawing the conclusion that it is permissible to punish the male even though the female may be equally culpable, because the female is hurt more?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well, it--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And that the male deserves to be punished for engaging in conduct that will hurt the other person more than it does himself.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --First of all, the--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Isn't that... isn't that generally the--

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Yes, it certainly is. It's--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: --Well how do you answer that?

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --Well first of all, the... the harm is only a potential harm, and certainly it occurs with some frequency, but does not certainly occur in every case. And, furthermore, at the time the teenage couple or whoever they are, decide to engage in sexual intercourse, the risks are equally apparent to the female as well as to the male and the female, quite voluntarily, as far as--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You're just saying she... the female is equally culpable, that's all you're saying.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: --I'm saying she is equally culpable because she can certainly appreciate the dangers and risks of a decision to engage in sexual intercourse. Just as much as the male. There is no showing that punishing the female for example, or punishing the male instead of the female, is any more effective than vice versa, really.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well the State is saying to the male, because the results of this conduct will fall more heavily on the female, you should be especially careful.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Well, as Justice Mosk stated, this goes to the consequences rather than the reason for the conduct and as such, is irrelevant in weighing the female's moral culpability.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Yes, but couldn't you say the same thing about OSHA, for instance, that the risks are possible, but the worker may not fall off the stepladder, but nonetheless, the government has the perfect right to say there's enough of a chance that he will that we're going to make sure it's safe.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Certainly, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, but as I understand the OSHA statutes, none of them classify on the basis of sex; none of them define conduct for males that is hazardous that is not hazardous for females.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well but they do... they do allow the legislature to take into consideration... that the legislature has taken into consideration possible risks as well as actual consequences.

turns.name: GREGORY F. JILKA
turns.text: Yes, that is certainly true.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I am Deputy Attorney General Sandy R. Kriegler, appearing today on behalf of the people of the State of California, real party in interest. I would like to discuss the constitutionality of California Penal Code Section 261.5, in light of the tests set forth by this Court for gender based classifications, in Craig v. Boren. Under Craig v. Boren, classifications based upon gender are constitutional if they serve important governmental objectives, and if the classification scheme is substantially related to the achievement of those objectives. This Court has recognized that classifications based upon gender are not invalid, per se. Indeed, where the sexes are not similarly situated or in those situations where society imposes special burdens upon females, different treatment is allowed. We believe that the classification under the statute before the Court today is based upon physiological differences which do not reflect the type of invidious discrimination which the Equal Protection Clause prohibits.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler, do prosecutors in California always press these as felonies?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well, where is the line of distinction? Here you have two young people about the same age, one year and 18 days apart, and you press it as a felony?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe it was pressed as a felony in this case due to the particular facts involved. And these particular facts are revealed in the appendix--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well I read the appendix thoroughly and I would say the particular facts would seem to cut the other way.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well Your Honor, the prosecutor obviously disagreed based upon the fact that the victim clearly testified, under oath at the preliminary hearing, that she submitted to the act of intercourse only after being slugged in the face 2 to 3 times with sufficient force to leave bruises. We believe that this case, while it is not perhaps sufficient to constitute a forcible rape, or at least the prosecutor who filed the charges did not feel so, it certainly approached that. And it was above the level of consensual intercourse, that one might normally expect to be involved in a situation involving two minors. Certainly, the use of force, I feel, was the crucial factor in the prosecutor's decision to file this case as a felony. I would point out, however, to the Court that the case was originally filed in Juvenile Court, and the Petitioner was found unfit for treatment under the Juvenile Court law and then it came to adult court. So the initial filing was not even as a felony, it was under our Juvenile Court law under which there are no felonies or misdemeanors; it is not a criminal branch of our judiciary. We believe that the important governmental interests served by Penal Code Section 261.5 are the deterrence of teenage pregnancy, the prevention of physical injury to minor females from sexual intercourse.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Kriegler, would either of those purposes be served any less well by a statute that was entirely neutral that applied to the female as well as the male?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor, particularly in this case. If our statute were gender neutral in this case, the victim, Sharon, would be, at least on the face of the statute, subject to prosecution. If she were subject to prosecution, first of all, she would be less likely to report the incident, because to do would be to incriminate herself.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Also, she might be less anxious to commit it, too, wouldn't she?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: She may, Your Honor, but in view of the--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I thought maybe--

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor. I would just point out that in view of the testimony that she gave under oath, she was not a willing participant in this act; she submitted--

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: --Well, who bought the liquor? Did you say she was not a willing participant--

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --She was not a willing participant.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: --Well doesn't the Appendix show that she began the intimacies?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Your Honor, she engaged in a certain amount of kissing, apparently, from the record, with the Defendant.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Making out, the term was used, and objected to and sustained.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe that was Mr. Jilka's term that he used in the preliminary hearing. However, it's clear that she did not wish to go further and proceed to an act of intercourse. And indeed, in that act she bore all the risks of the potential pregnancy, she was not willing to engage in the act, at least as far as the record that's before this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And you say he was a perfect stranger?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe the record indicates she may have known the Petitioner.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: When you say the record may show, doesn't the record actually show that she rode around in the police car to pick him out on the street?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: That--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Doesn't the record show that?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well you don't go around looking for people that you know, do you? You go to their home or where ever they are to pick them out.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well all I'm saying is, she may have been familiar with the individual, I don't know that she was a personal friend or a close acquaintance--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, Mr. Kriegler, if the issue before us is the constitutional validity on its face of this California legislation, what in the world do the facts of this case have to do with it?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well we believe the statute is constitutional on its face for the reasons set forth by the California Supreme Court. It is Petitioner who has come before this Court urging that the statute is unconstitutional as applied to him because he is also a minor. That is the only reason that the as-applied argument is being made.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But if the facts have any relevancy at all, they bear only on the decision of the prosecutor as to whether to prosecute on a felony or a misdemeanor, isn't that so? And that issue is not before us. Only the constitutionality, as Justice Stewart has said several times, on its face, is the issue here.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: We agree. And our only point would be that insofar as Petitioner is pursuing the argument that the statute is unconstitutional as applied to him as a 17-year old, he cannot have it both ways; he cannot straddle the fence and say it's unconstitutional to apply this to me as a minor, on the other hand ignoring the specific conduct that he engaged in--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well why not? It's perfectly apparent on the face of the statute that it would apply to a consensual act between 17-year olds.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --That is not the facts before this Court. The statute on its face applies to all forms of--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: But do you not defend the constitutionality of the statute as applied to a consensual act between two 17-year olds of opposite sexes who are equally culpable? Yes, you do.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, we certainly do.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: They why do we have to argue about anything else?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Because it's important to point out, in this case, the two 17-year olds were not equally culpable.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well why is it important if you're defending the statute-- --How do we know that? You're just-- --instead of the prosecution? --and there's been no trial and no finding of fact, so we don't know whether that's true or not.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, there has been no trial. However, whether the parties are engaged in the type of conduct that occurred in this case or as Your Honor suggests, whether we view the statute on its face, we submit that the statute is nonetheless constitutional because it serves these two important governmental interests that I have mentioned and--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: You say that the reason it serves it better than a sex neutral statute is that after the act has been committed, then there's a greater likelihood that it will be reported by the female?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Right--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: But in terms of the deterrence of the conduct itself, is it not clear that if the penalty applied to both, there would be less likelihood of the risk-creating conduct taking place? Right.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I don't think that necessarily follows. Females have--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: You don't think the female should ever be punished for engaging in this kind of conduct as a deterrent to further examples of the same kind of conduct?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well I think the state might... the state legislature might, in the exercise of its sound judgment,--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Would it be rational for a parent to say it's perfectly all right for my sons to do this, but it's wrong for my daughters to do this and therefore I will punish my sons but not my daughters? That's what the state is doing.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --It would depend upon the circumstances, Your Honor, and the act--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well it's always a risk-creating act, isn't it?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --It is, but the risks are borne uniquely by the female. But it might--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: But it takes two participants every time the act is performed, doesn't it?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, it does, it certainly does.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And if it's consensual, why should one be punished and not the other one?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: One should be punished because the California legislature has determined that the female bears these extraordinary risks from the act of intercourse; because of these extraordinary risks which Petitioner has conceded in it's brief, to be compelling state interests, the legislature has reasonably concluded, we believe, that the sexes are not similarly situated.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Has the legislature reasonably concluded that those risks have been sufficient to deter females from voluntarily engaging in this kind of conduct?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, the question of deterrence is one that is very difficult to answer; we don't know how many people have or have not been deterred by the existence of this statute, or any other statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Not very many have. The point is, must be, I think, that the risk of pregnancy is a deterrence with respect to the female, and--

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I submit--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: --and in order to... and there is no such deterrence with respect to the male. And in order to deter the male this statute is necessary, applicable to males who indulge in this conduct, but not females because they are equivalently deterred by the risk of pregnancy, or the fear of pregnancy, isn't that your argument?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, it is, Mr. Justice Stewart.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, Mr. Attorney General, the first statute was passed in 1850, right?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Marshall.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And it applied to girls 10 years old, right?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Is there any literature that shows that there was a rash of pregnancies among 9 year olds in 1850?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: No. We are quite confident that the statute as originally enacted in 1950 was intended to prevent physical injury to minor females, 9 year olds certainly, at that time--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well when did it change to pregnancy?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well the statute--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And I'm going to ask for a citation when you answer.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Fine. The statute, Mr. Justice Marshall, was amended incrementally, at three stages: the age of the protection for the female raised to 14, 16, and eventually 18, in 1913. We believe that while there is no concrete legislative history to support the reasoning for those increases, certainly one inference that could reasonably be drawn as the age of the victim increased the possibility of pregnancy occurring was more frequent. Now--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Have you ever heard of anything more speculative than that in your life?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Your Honor, there are some additional points I would like to make in regard to the question of legislative intent, if I might?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: In 1967, California passed its Therapeutic Abortion Act, and that is cited in our brief. And that of course was well prior to the time that this Court enunciated the rule that abortions were constitutionally mandated... must be made available. In that act, the one category of victim that was specifically named as being unconditionally eligible for abortion was statutory rape victims under our former statutory rape law; California Penal Code Section 261 Subdivision 1. Because that particular category of victim under Section 261 Subdivision 1, was specifically singled out as being eligible for abortion, we believe that it's very clear at least at that time, the legislature perceived the purpose of the statutory rape law, at least in part, as being intended to deter teenage pregnancies.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Did it change its statute?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. Subsequently the statute was repealed--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Subsequently?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, in 1970, as a matter of fact, the present edition of the statute, Penal Code Section 261.5, was enacted, it was taken out of the former rape section, and it was called unlawful sexual intercourse. We believe that since that occurred three years after the legislative deferration of... the connection between statutory rape and teenage pregnancy--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Three years later?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It wasn't the same legislature, either.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, it was not the same legislature. But certainly, I think, that is the most recent indication, at least at that time, of what the legislatures' thinking was in regards to the purpose of the statutory rape law. I would also point--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Mr Kriegler, really, I don't... I either didn't understand in reading your briefs, or your opponent's briefs, why you run away from the idea that the purpose of this statute is to protect the chastity of minor females and don't face up to the fact that your opponent argues that California is constitutionally prohibited from passing such legislation unless it also protects... legislation to protect the chastity of minor males.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well first of all--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's the argument.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I don't think that is the intent of the legislature in this case. First of all, because there's--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: What difference does it make?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --no chastity requirement. Well, there's no chastity requirement in our statute, number one.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well why then, all these statistics on the great rise in pregnancies of unmarried teenagers, 12-13-14 years old, if that wasn't related to the legislatures' purpose?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, certainly there is a direct correlation between the frequency of intercourse today and the resulting pregnancies; I don't think there's any dispute about that. My point is--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: According to the opinion of others I thought that California's position was that the California legislature has a right to try to protect minor children, females, from the whole range of consequences of this kind of conduct?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --That is correct, and we also protect minor males. I don't want to leave this Court with the false--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well in this case, you protect only females, because it applies only when females are victims.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Because of--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But the argument is that California is constitutionally disabled from enacting any such legislation and that it is constitutionally required to protect also minor males, if it protects minor females. Now that's a very odd argument, and you haven't done it at all.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well, our response is that California is not constitutionally required to treat dissimilarly situated sexes the same way. We have established that the very narrow category of victim in this case is in need of special protection. Minor males are not in need of the same protection. That is not to say, however, that minor males do not get protection under the California laws.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Isn't that because there's a greater moral stigma to this act if it's performed by an unmarried female than by an unmarried male?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I don't think it is necessarily a moral stigma, it may be a basis of... of--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, does California, is it the policy of the state of California to place greater importance on the chastity of the female than on the chastity of the male?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I don't think--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: In moral terms?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I don't think it turns upon the question of chastity, Your Honor. I think it turns upon--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Then Mr. Justice Stewart is suggesting you should face up to that, and I'm wondering whether, in facing up to that, you are contending there is a distinction in the, in the morality of the act, depending on the sex of the person who participated. Or that's really... you don't have to contend that. Just-- --Or you rely entirely on pregnancy-- --California legislature determined to protect the chastity of minor females, not of all people of all ages, or of both sexes, and your opponent's argument is that California is constitutionally required, if it protects minor females to at least protect minor males. We don't have here the question of whether it also is constitutionally required to protect the chastity of males and females of all ages but that would be the next case. You certainly must argue... you certainly must argue that the consequences, apart from any moral issue, the consequences socially, economically and in various other ways, are much greater on the girl, is that not so?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --That is the entire basis of our argument.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But you haven't been making that argument, Mr. Kriegler. Yes, you've hardly had time, have you? No. Or a chance.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well, now, go ahead and make it now, if you are ready to.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Once the statutory purposes of deterring these teenage pregnancies, and as Mr. Chief Justice has, thankfully, pointed out... the consequences of these pregnancies are just disastrous on minor females. Eight out of ten never complete high school; 60 percent end up on welfare within 2 to 5 years. The consequences are medically disastrous, the--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Is it your argument that there would be less of a deterrent if it was also unlawful for the female to engage in sexual intercourse at this age; that that would be a lesser deterrent to pregnancy?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I think there would be a problem involved in it, and I started to answer, I think, the same question a little bit earlier and I didn't quite get to complete my answer. First--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Enforcement problem, you've identified that.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well, it even goes beyond the problem of reporting. If the statute were gender neutral, the female would be culpable also as an aider and abettor, or a principal, in this case. That creates problems of who is the state going to prosecute; the male or the female? Well that's a judgment call on the part of prosecutors. You start getting into the question of is this particular case a discriminatory prosecution? So we have a hearing in which this minor female who is supposed to be protected by the statute, goes through all the facts again and we decide whether it's a discriminatory prosecution or not. But even if we were to say that she should be protected under the statute, that creates another problem in California, because she, as an accomplice or an aider or abettor, must have her testimony corroborated under California Penal Code Section 111.1. And under that section, her testimony standing by itself won't be sufficient to constitute substantial evidence for a conviction under California law. These acts are, by and large, committed in privacy. This would be a very substantial problem for the state of California to enforce its law, if we had a gender neutral statute. When we add to that the fact that in the great majority of these cases, the perpetrator is the adult... is an adult. We have cited statistics in our brief which show that it's a very rare case indeed that a minor male is prosecuted. Perhaps only one--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: But in terms of the actual conduct you seek to deter, you don't have statistics about that, do you? I mean, isn't there a large volume of conduct in which the participants are both young persons?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --I'm sure there is, Your Honor, yes. I don't think we can deny that fact.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And you think that you would not be as equally effective in deterring that conduct if you also imposed a penalty on the female for engaging in it?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well it may or may not be--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Because of these enforcement problems you describe?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well it may or may not be the case. Many states--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well if it's not the case then you have no rational basis for the discrimination. You may have a rational basis for the prohibition, but you have to justify why do we make it just as to one and not the other?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well I think it goes beyond the enforcement problems. There I come back to our original point, the statute is based upon deterring the particular harmful--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Let me give you an example which I've reflected on. Supposing you had a law that made it reckless driving to drive on a motorcycle at excessive rate of speed. And you said well, if a person doesn't wear a helmet, he'll suffer greater harm if he has an accident, so we will exclude people who do not wear helmets from the prohibition against reckless driving. Under your rationale that would be a perfectly reasonable exclusion, is that not right?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Because they suffer a greater risk of harm, therefore you don't need to deter them with legal sanctions?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Well, I don't think that necessarily follows.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And that's your argument about the female; she suffers a greater risk of harm, and therefore, she should not be deterred. She should know intelligently, no matter how young she is, that she should not do this.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, this statute, Your Honor, is consistent with all of the California laws which prohibit sexual offenses with minors, whether they be male or females. Under California law where the minor is protected from acts involving lewd or lascivious conduct, or annoying or molesting that child or other acts of sex perversion, it matters not whether the minor who engages in those acts with the adult counterpart is acting consensually or not. The statute is there for the protection of that particular individual, because of the consequences of the act. And it doesn't matter whether the act is consensual or not; the severe consequences to the minor victim in all of our California statutes, are recognized, because the minors are not treated as being culpable for the crime that's committed. When the female in this case is properly viewed as the victim and is properly viewed as suffering disparate consequences from the act of intercourse, none of which whatever are borne by the male who engages in the act of intercourse, we believe that the statute does not contain--

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Is it part of your position that the male participant never suffers any adverse consequences other than legal prohibition?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --We are unaware of any substantial adverse consequences to the minor male.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: There's financial responsibility, sometimes. There's physical harm, sometimes, isn't there?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: We are unaware of any--

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well how about venereal disease?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --That is a potential harm that both suffer.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: To either one?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: How about an irate father?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: That may be a more practical consideration, I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And some liability for support, eventually?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well I think that's pretty tenuous, particularly in this case, where, you know, we have a minor male participant. I really--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Could I ask you... could I ask you, suppose a young man, a teenager, is on trial under this statute and he's tried before the judge, and he defends on the ground that the statute as applied to him is unconstitutional because in this case the woman was the aggressor, and that he surely shouldn't be punished in those circumstances and the judge finds yes, the woman was the aggressor, and is much culpable and blameworthy in the case, but nevertheless says this statute requires it and this statute is constitutional. I suppose you must defend the statute, even in those circumstances?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, we would, for the same reasons that I stated previously. But I would like to point out, and emphasize this fact most strongly to the Court, after all the research that has been done in this case, Petitioner has yet to cite to a single instance where the statute has been applied in an unfair manner as suggested by Mr. Justice White's question. We believe that the statute as applied in this case was clearly applied to the sexual aggressor. The cases that are decided in California under our--

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well but that's just avoidance, you would say, even if it were applied every day in those kinds of circumstances that I gave you, it would be constitutional?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Yes, Your Honor, definitely it would. I would simply point out, though--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: When you talk about the sexual aggressor, would that be... a person that plied somebody with liquor?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --Could possibly, Your Honor, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And isn't that what happened in this case?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: I believe the girl drank her own liquor in this case, Your Honor. I don't think she plied anybody with liquor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well she... she drank it herself?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Yes, she did, certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But if he'd given it to her, he'd have been the aggressor?

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Well, we don't... we don't base our--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I hope not.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: --on that fact, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well I come back again to where we've been before. We're not dealing with this case of the statute as applied, but on its face, and nothing else.

turns.name: SANDY R. KRIEGLER
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor. We would strongly urge this Court to recognize the severe consequences that befall only the teenage female participant in an act of intercourse, regardless of the age of the male participant. And with the Court being aware, as it is, of the consequences of teenage pregnancy, I don't need to remind the Court certainly of the statements made in earlier cases. I know, Mr. Justice Powell speaking for the Court in Bellotti v. Baird certainly recognized that the consequences of pregnancy, severe as they are to an adult woman, are even more severe to the unwed, teenage mother. California legislature has recognized the disastrous consequences of teenage pregnancy and sexual abuse of minor females in the state of California. The problem is conceded by Petitioner to be one of monumental proportions. The state of California has made the valid judgment that in view of the risks which are borne only by the minor female, none of which are suffered by the minor male, that criminal sanctions should be employed as it is, under California Penal Code Section 261.5. We ask this Court to leave intact California Penal Code Section 261.5 because of the degree of protection it provides to this particular class of individual is so needing in that protection. We submit that the judgment of the California Supreme Court should be affirmed. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen. The case is submitted.
